sections.label: Section 7.5
sections.original: συμποσίου τε χάριν κᾶδός τε τιμάσαις νέον , ἐν δὲ φίλων
sections.translation: honoring the grace of the symposium and the new [*] marriage-bond , and thereby , in the presence of his friends , makes him enviable for his harmonious marriage-bed ;

sections.label: Section 7.7
sections.original: καὶ ἐγὼ νέκταρ χυτόν , Μοισᾶν δόσιν , ἀεθλοφόροις
sections.translation: I too , sending to victorious men poured nectar , the gift of the Muses , the sweet fruit of my mind , I try to win the gods ’ favor

sections.label: Section 7.10
sections.original: Οὐλυμπίᾳ Πυθοῖ τε νικώντεσσιν · ὁ δʼ ὄλβιος , ὃν φᾶμαι κατέχοντʼ ἀγαθαί .
sections.translation: for those men who were victors at Olympia and at Pytho , That man is prosperous , who is encompassed by good reports . Grace , which causes life to flourish , looks with favor now on one man , now on another , with both the sweet-singing lyre and the full-voiced notes of flutes .

sections.label: Section 7.13
sections.original: καί νυν ὑπʼ ἀμφοτέρων σὺν Διαγόρᾳ κατέβαν τὰν ποντίαν
sections.translation: And now , with the music of flute and lyre alike I have come to land with Diagoras , singing the sea-child of Aphrodite and bride of Helios , Rhodes ,

sections.label: Section 7.15
sections.original: εὐθυμάχαν ὄφρα πελώριον ἄνδρα παρʼ Ἀλφεῷ στεφανωσάμενον
sections.translation: so that I may praise this straight-fighting , tremendous man who had himself crowned beside the Alpheus and near Castalia , as a recompense for his boxing , and also his father Damagetus , a man pleasing to Justice , living on the island of three cities near the foreland of spacious Asia , among Argive spearmen .

sections.label: Section 7.20
sections.original: ἐθελήσω τοῖσιν ἐξ ἀρχᾶς ἀπὸ Τλαπολέμου
sections.translation: I shall want to proclaim my message for them , the widely powerful race of Heracles , and tell correctly from the beginning , from Tlepolemus , the story that concerns all . For , on the father ’ s side , they boast descent from Zeus , while , on the mother ’ s , they are descendants of Amyntor , through Astydameia . But around the minds of men

sections.label: Section 7.25
sections.original: ἀναρίθμητοι κρέμανται · τοῦτο δʼ ἀμάχανον εὑρεῖν ,
sections.translation: countless errors loom ; and this is impossible to discover :

sections.label: Section 7.26
sections.original: ὅ τι νῦν ἐν καὶ τελευτᾷ φέρτατον ἀνδρὶ τυχεῖν .
sections.translation: what is best to happen to a man , now and in the end . For indeed , striking Licymnius , the bastard brother of Alcmena , with a staff of hard olive-wood as he came out of the chamber of Midea ,

sections.label: Section 7.30
sections.original: τᾶσδέ ποτε χθονὸς οἰκιστὴρ χολωθείς . αἱ δὲ φρενῶν ταραχαὶ
sections.translation: the founder of this land once killed that man , in anger . Disturbances of the mind lead astray even a wise man . Tlepolemus went and sought the god ’ s oracle .

sections.label: Section 8.1
sections.original: μᾶτερ ὦ χρυσοστεφάνων ἀέθλων , Οὐλυμπία ,
sections.translation: Mother of golden-crowned contests , Olympia , queen of truth ! where prophets , judging from burnt sacrifices , inquire of Zeus of the flashing thunderbolt , if he has any message to give concerning men

sections.label: Section 8.5
sections.original: μαιομένων μεγάλαν
sections.translation: whose spirits are seeking to attain great excellence and a breathing-space from toils .

sections.label: Section 8.8
sections.original: 10 ἄνεται δὲ πρὸς χάριν εὐσεβίας ἀνδρῶν λιταῖς .
sections.translation: Accomplishment is granted to the prayers of men in gratitude for their piety . Well-wooded grove of Pisa beside the Alpheus ,

sections.label: Section 8.10
sections.original: τόνδε κῶμον καὶ στεφαναφορίαν δέξαι . μέγα τοι κλέος αἰεί ,
sections.translation: welcome this victory-procession and the garland we bring to the victor ; the man who is attended by your splendid prize of honor has great glory forever . Some good things come to one man , some to another ; with the favor of the gods , there are many paths of success .

sections.label: Section 8.15
sections.original: Τιμόσθενες , ὔμμε δʼ ἐκλάρωσεν πότμος
sections.translation: Timosthenes , fortune has allotted you and your brother to the care of your ancestor Zeus , who made you renowned at Nemea , and made Alcimedon an Olympic victor beside the hill of Cronus . He was beautiful to look at , and his deeds did not belie his beauty

sections.label: Section 8.20
sections.original: ἐξένεπε κρατέων πάλᾳ δολιχήρετμον Αἴγιναν πάτραν ·
sections.translation: when by his victory in wrestling he had Aegina with her long oars proclaimed as his fatherland . There the savior Themis , seated beside Zeus the god of hospitality , is honored

sections.label: Section 8.23
sections.original: 30 ἔξοχʼ ἀνθρώπων . ὅ τι γὰρ πολὺ καὶ πολλᾷ ῥέπῃ ,
sections.translation: more than among all other men . For when there is a heavy weight in the balance that sways many ways , to judge with a straight mind and not inopportunely

sections.label: Section 8.25
sections.original: δυσπαλές · τεθμὸς δέ τις ἀθανάτων καὶ τάνδʼ ἁλιερκέα χώραν
sections.translation: is a difficult struggle . But some ordinance of the immortals set up as a divine pillar for visitors of all kinds this sea-girt land — and may the dawning time to come never tire of fulfilling this —

sections.label: Section 8.30
sections.original: 40 Δωριεῖ λαῷ ταμιευομέναν ἐξ Αἰακοῦ ·
sections.translation: guarded by the Dorian people since the time of Aeacus , whom wide-ruling Poseidon and the son of Leto , when they were about to build the crown of walls to encircle Ilium , summoned as a fellow worker ; for it was fated that when war arose ,

sections.label: Section 9.1
sections.original: τὸ μὲν Ἀρχιλόχου μέλος
sections.translation: The resounding strain of Archilochus , the swelling thrice-repeated song of triumph , sufficed to lead Epharmostus to the hill of Cronus , in victory-procession with his dear companions .

sections.label: Section 9.5
sections.original: ἀλλὰ νῦν ἑκαταβόλων Μοισᾶν ἀπὸ τόξων
sections.translation: But now , from the bow of the Muses who , shooting from afar , send a shower of such arrows of song as these on Zeus of the red lightning-bolt and on the sacred height of Elis , which once the Lydian hero Pelops

sections.label: Section 9.10
sections.original: ἐξάρατο κάλλιστον ἕδνον Ἱπποδαμείας ·
sections.translation: won as the very fine dowry of Hippodameia .

sections.label: Section 9.11
sections.original: πτερόεντα δʼ ἵει γλυκὺν
sections.translation: And shoot a winged sweet arrow to Pytho ; for your words will not fall to the ground , short of the mark , when you trill the lyre in honor of the wrestling of the man from renowned Opus . Praise Opus and her son ;

sections.label: Section 9.15
sections.original: ἃν Θέμις θυγάτηρ τέ οἱ σώτειρα λέλογχεν
sections.translation: praise her whom Themis and her glorious daughter , the savior Eunomia , have received under their protection ; she flourishes with excellence beside your stream , Castalia , and beside the Alpheus . From there the choicest garlands

sections.label: Section 9.20
sections.original: Λοκρῶν ἐπαείροντι ματέρʼ ἀγλαόδενδρον .
sections.translation: glorify the famous mother-city of the Locrians with her splendid trees .

sections.label: Section 9.21
sections.original: ἐγὼ δέ τοι φίλαν πόλιν
sections.translation: I am lighting up that dear city with fiery songs , and more swiftly than a spirited horse or a winged ship

sections.label: Section 9.25
sections.original: ἀγγελίαν πέμψω ταύταν ,
sections.translation: I will send that message everywhere , so surely as I , by some destined skill , am cultivating the exquisite garden of the Graces ; for they are the givers of delight , but men become brave and skillful by divine will .

sections.label: Section 9.29
sections.original: ἐγένοντʼ · ἐπεὶ ἀντία
sections.translation: For

sections.label: Section 9.30
sections.original: πῶς ἂν τριόδοντος Ἡρακλέης σκύταλον τίναξε χερσίν ,
sections.translation: how could Heracles have wielded his club against the trident , when Poseidon took his stand to guard Pylos , and pressed him hard , and Phoebus pressed him hard , attacking with his silver bow ; nor did Hades keep his staff unmoved , with which he leads mortal bodies down to the hollow path

sections.label: Section 10.1
sections.original: τὸν Ὀλυμπιονίκαν ἀνάγνωτέ μοι
sections.translation: Read me the name of the Olympic victor , the son of Archestratus , where it has been written in my mind . For I owed him a sweet song , and I have forgotten . But come , Muse , you and the daughter of Zeus , unforgetting Truth : with the hand that puts things right ,

sections.label: Section 10.5
sections.original: ἐρύκετον ψευδέων
sections.translation: keep from me the blame for lying , for wronging my friend .

sections.label: Section 10.7
sections.original: ἕκαθεν γὰρ ἐπελθὼν ὁ μέλλων χρόνος
sections.translation: Approaching from far away , the future has arrived and made me ashamed of my deep debt . Still , payment with interest has a way of dissolving the bitter reproach of men .

sections.label: Section 10.10
sections.original: ὅπα κῦμα κατακλύσσει ῥέον ,
sections.translation: Now , just as the flowing wave overwhelms the rolling pebble , so shall I pay my account in full , in gratitude and friendship .

sections.label: Section 10.13
sections.original: νέμει γὰρ Ἀτρέκεια πόλιν Λοκρῶν Ζεφυρίων ,
sections.translation: For unswerving Exactitude rules the city of the Western Locrians , and Calliope is important to them , and bronze-armored Ares .

sections.label: Section 10.15
sections.original: 20 καὶ χάλκεος Ἄρης . τράπε δὲ Κύκνεια μάχα καὶ ὑπέρβιον
sections.translation: Battle with Cycnus set back even Heracles , strong and violent ; let Hagesidamus , victorious as a boxer at Olympia , offer thanks to Ilas , just as Patroclus did to Achilles .

sections.label: Section 10.20
sections.original: θήξαις δέ κε φύντʼ ἀρετᾷ ποτὶ
sections.translation: With the help of a god , one man can sharpen another who is born for excellence , and encourage him to tremendous achievement .

sections.label: Section 10.22
sections.original: ἄπονον δʼ ἔλαβον χάρμα παῦροί τινες ,
sections.translation: Without toil only a few have attained joy , a light of life above all labors . The laws of Zeus urge me to sing of that extraordinary contest-place which Heracles founded by the ancient tomb of Pelops

sections.label: Section 10.25
sections.original: βωμῶν ἑξάριθμον ἐκτίσσατο ,
sections.translation: with its six altars , after he killed Cteatus , the flawless son of Poseidon

sections.label: Section 10.28
sections.original: πέφνε δʼ Εὔρυτον , ὡς Αὐγέαν λάτριον
sections.translation: and Eurytus too , with a will to exact from the unwilling Augeas , strong and violent , the wages for his menial labor .

sections.label: Section 10.30
sections.original: πράσσοιτο , λόχμαισι δὲ δοκεύσαις ὑπὸ Κλεωνᾶν δάμασε καὶ κείνους Ἡρακλέης ἐφʼ ὁδῷ ,
sections.translation: Heracles lay in wait in the thicket below Cleonae , and in his turn overcame those men by the roadside ; for once before those arrogant Moliones had destroyed his Tirynthian army , when it was encamped in the valley of Elis ,

sections.label: Section 11.1
sections.original: ἔστιν ἀνθρώποις ἀνέμων ὅτε πλείστα
sections.translation: There is a time when men ’ s need for winds is the greatest , and a time for waters from the sky , the rainy offspring of clouds . But when anyone is victorious through his toil , then honey-voiced odes

sections.label: Section 11.5
sections.original: ὑστέρων ἀρχὰ λόγων
sections.translation: become the foundation for future fame , and a faithful pledge for great deeds of excellence .

sections.label: Section 11.7
sections.original: ἀφθόνητος δʼ αἶνος Ὀλυμπιονίκαις
sections.translation: This praise is dedicated to Olympian victors , without stint . My tongue wants to foster such themes ;

sections.label: Section 11.10
sections.original: 10 ἐκ θεοῦ δʼ ἀνὴρ σοφαῖς ἀνθεῖ πραπίδεσσιν ὁμοίως .
sections.translation: but it is by the gift of a god that a man flourishes with a skillful mind , as with anything else . For the present rest assured , Hagesidamus son of Archestratus : for the sake of your boxing victory ,

sections.label: Section 11.13
sections.original: κόσμον ἐπὶ στεφάνῳ χρυσέας ἐλαίας
sections.translation: I shall loudly sing a sweet song , an adornment for your garland of golden olive ,

sections.label: Section 11.15
sections.original: Ζεφυρίων , Λοκρῶν γενεὰν ἀλέγων .
sections.translation: while I honor the race of the Western Locrians . There , Muses , join in the victory-song ; I shall pledge my word to you that we will find there a race that does not repel the stranger , or is inexperienced in fine deeds , but one that is wise and warlike too . For

sections.label: Section 11.20
sections.original: 20 ἐμφυὲς οὔτʼ αἴθων ἀλώπηξ
sections.translation: neither the fiery fox nor loud-roaring lions change their nature .

sections.label: Section 12.1
sections.original: λίσσομαι , παῖ Ζηνὸς Ἐλευθερίου ,
sections.translation: I entreat you , child of Zeus the Deliverer , saving Fortune , keep protecting Himera , and make her powerful . For by your favor swift ships are steered on the sea , and on dry land rushing battles

sections.label: Section 12.5
sections.original: κἀγοραὶ βουλαφόροι . αἵ γε μὲν ἀνδρῶν
sections.translation: and assemblies where counsel is given . But men ’ s expectations are often tossed up and then back down , as they cleave the waves of vain falsehood .

sections.label: Section 12.7
sections.original: 10 σύμβολον δʼ οὔ πώ τις ἐπιχθονίων
sections.translation: Never yet has any man on earth found a reliable token of what will happen from the gods . Our understanding of the future is blind .

sections.label: Section 12.10
sections.original: πολλὰ δʼ ἀνθρώποις παρὰ γνώμαν ἔπεσεν ,
sections.translation: And therefore many things fall out for men contrary to their judgement , bringing to some reversal of delight , while others , having encountered grievous storms , in a short time exchange their troubles for high success .

sections.label: Section 12.13
sections.original: υἱὲ Φιλάνορος , ἤτοι καὶ τεά κεν ,
sections.translation: Son of Philanor , truly , like a cock that fights at home , even

sections.label: Section 12.15
sections.original: ἀκλεὴς τιμὰ κατεφυλλορόησε ποδῶν ,
sections.translation: the fame of your swift feet would have shed its leaves ingloriously beside your native hearth , if hostile civil strife had not deprived you of your Cnossian fatherland . But as things are , Ergoteles , having been crowned with garlands at Olympia , and twice from Pytho , and at the Isthmus , you exalt the hot baths of the Nymphs , while keeping company with them beside your own fields .

sections.label: Section 13.1
sections.original: τρισολυμπιονίκαν
sections.translation: While I praise a house that has been three times victorious at Olympia , gentle to her own citizens , and hospitable to strangers , I shall recognize prosperous Corinth ,

sections.label: Section 13.5
sections.original: πρόθυρον Ποτειδᾶνος , ἀγλαόκουρον .
sections.translation: the portal of Isthmian Poseidon , glorious in her young men . There dwell Eunomia [*] and her sisters , the secure foundation of cities : Dike , [*] and Eirene , [*] who was raised together with her , the guardians of wealth for men , the golden daughters of wise Themis . [*]

sections.label: Section 13.9
sections.original: ἐθέλοντι δʼ ἀλέξειν
sections.translation: They are resolute in repelling

sections.label: Section 13.10
sections.original: Ὕβριν , Κόρου ματέρα θρασύμυθον .
sections.translation: Hybris , [*] the bold-tongued mother of Koros , [*] I have fine things to tell , and straightforward boldness urges my tongue to speak . It is impossible to conceal one ’ s inborn nature . As for you , sons of Aletes , often the Seasons have sent you victorious splendor

sections.label: Section 13.15
sections.original: 20 ἄκραις ἀρεταῖς ὑπερελθόντων ἱεροῖς ἐν ἀέθλοις ,
sections.translation: for your consummate excellence when you won in sacred contests , and often into the hearts of men

sections.label: Section 13.17
sections.original: ὧραι πολυάνθεμοι ἀρχαῖα σοφίσμαθʼ . ἅπαν δʼ εὑρόντος ἔργον .
sections.translation: the Seasons rich in flowers have cast ancient inventiveness . But the fame for every work is due to its inventor . Whence did the graces of Dionysus first come to light , with the ox-driving dithyramb ?

sections.label: Section 13.20
sections.original: τίς γὰρ ἱππείοις ἐν ἔντεσσιν μέτρα ,
sections.translation: Who invented the bridle for the harness of horses , or placed the double king of birds on top of the temples of gods ? And in Corinth the sweet-breathing Muse blossoms , and also Ares , with the deadly spears of young men .

sections.label: Section 13.24
sections.original: ὕπατʼ εὐρὺ ἀνάσσων
sections.translation: Highest lord
